agent1_snippets.title: Adenoid Cystic Cancer -- Medical Oncology
agent1_snippets.content: Because ACCs do not have a fast turnover rate, systemic chemotherapy does not confer benefit generally, and therefore a standard chemotherapy regimen is not a part of treatment for ACCs. [1] However, the consensus remains to reserve chemotherapy for palliative purposes in patients with symptomatic metastatic disease, recurrent disease, or rapidly progressing tumors not amenable to surgery. Studies have also been done to look at possible molecular targets for immunotherapy agents, but results have been disappointing. Clinical trials looking at c-KIT (an onco-receptor overexpressed by ACCs) as a potential target for the c-KIT inhibitor, imatinib, showed disappointing results. [14] [15] Similarly, drugs targeting the EGFR and HER-2 receptor families did not show any clinical benefit. [16] [17] [18] However, a recent phase II trial looking at the tyrosine kinase inhibitor axitinib in patients with incurable recurrent/metastatic ACC showed that 66.7% of patients had tumor shrinkage and 9.1% of patients had a partial response suggesting that patients with 4q12 amplified ACCs may benefit from tyrosine kinase inhibitors. [19] NTRK inhibitors such as larotrectinib should be considered in NTRK mutations in the absence of other available options in recurrent or metastatic tumors, as approved by the FDA.

agent1_snippets.title: Evinacumab -- Indications
agent1_snippets.content: The diagnosis of HoFH is clinical; genetic testing should be performed if readily available. Possessing two mutant alleles for LDLR , Apo[b] , PCSK9 , or LDLR adaptor protein gene locus 1 is considered a positive test for HoFH. Clinically, an LDL-C ≥400 mg/dL and one or both parents having clinically diagnosed FH, positive genetic testing for a known LDL-C–raising ( LDLR , PCSK9 , Apo[b] ) gene defect, or autosomal-recessive FH indicates a patient has HoFH. An LDL-C >560 mg/dL or LDL-C >400 mg/dL with either aortic valve disease or xanthomas at 20 years of age or younger could also meet the HoFH diagnosis criteria.

agent1_snippets.title: Polyglandular Autoimmune Syndrome Type I -- Etiology
agent1_snippets.content: Autoimmune polyglandular syndrome type 1 is due to mutations in the AIRE gene located in the short arm of chromosome 21. Inheritance of APS-1 is in an autosomal recessive manner. The AIRE gene codes for a protein named autoimmune regulator. There are over ver 60 recognized different mutations in the AIRE gene, which might explain the variability in presentation and natural course of the disease. Over 95% of patients with APS-1 have 1 of the 2 most common mutations: arginine substitution at position 257 and 13 base pair deletion in exon 8. [2] [3]

agent1_snippets.title: Gliomas -- Medical Oncology
agent1_snippets.content: As many pediatric low-grade gliomas have genomic alterations in the mitogen-activated protein kinase pathway, studies have focused on targeted therapies involving this pathway. The phase 2 FIREFLY-1 trial assessed the efficacy of the RAF inhibitor tovorafenib in patients who suffered from relapse or refractory low-grade gliomas with BRAF alterations. [22] This study demonstrated an overall response rate of 67% and 51% when assessed using the Response Assessment in Neuro-Oncology (RANO) for high-grade gliomas (HGG) and Response Assessment in Pediatric Neuro-Oncology (RAPNO) criteria, respectively. Furthermore, the median time to treatment response was 3.0 months and 5.3 months per RANO-HGG and RAPNO criteria, respectively. These findings provide encouraging evidence for another treatment option for patients with relapsed or refractory low-grade gliomas. Several other studies have shown promising results with chemotherapy-directed treatment for diffuse midline gliomas, H3K27M altered. [23] [24]

agent1_snippets.title: Ataxia-Telangiectasia -- Treatment / Management -- Future Treatments
agent1_snippets.content: Antioxidants, antisense morpholino oligonucleotides, aminoglycoside antibiotics that can affect ATM protein function, and different small molecules targeting the ATM gene to address tumorigenesis have been tested. Most of these new therapeutic approaches are still under investigation. [53] Therapeutic trials are ongoing to test the efficacy of dexamethasone treatment through innovative delivery routes, such as patients’ autologous red blood cells loaded with dexamethasone (the ATTeST study). Recent studies also suggest that very low glucocorticoid doses may help improve A-T neurological symptomatology in some patients with A-T. [54] Moreover, short-term betamethasone treatment appears transiently to benefit patients with ataxia telangiectasia; however, long-term benefits and risks should be carefully considered since an adverse effect of transient adrenal dysfunction was observed in all cases. [55]

agent1_snippets.title: Ocular Futility and End of Sight Care -- Etiology -- Advanced Age-related Macular Degeneration (ARMD)
agent1_snippets.content: The benefit of continued injections is questionable in patients with neovascular ARMD with a significant atrophic component. One retrospective study suggested that continuing anti-VEGF injections after achieving morphologic and functional stability does not necessarily prevent future exudative changes. [6] This raises the question of when to stop injections, which has no clear answer in the literature. The general practice is to stop anti-VEGF injections when there is a sizeable disciform scar, even if there is a recurrence of fluid or subretinal hemorrhage. However, individual ophthalmologists may differ in their approach.

agent2_snippets.content: Icatibant is classified among the drugs used to treat hereditary angioedema, a rare genetic disorder characterized by episodic swelling of the skin and mucosal tissues. As a bradykinin B2‑receptor antagonist, icatibant specifically blocks the action of bradykinin, a key mediator of the swelling attacks in hereditary angioedema, thereby providing rapid relief of symptoms during acute episodes. Inclusion of icatibant in the therapeutic armamentarium underscores its role as a targeted pharmacologic option for managing this condition and improving patient outcomes.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 75
agent2_snippets.content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subclass of protein‑kinase inhibitors classified as antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, comprise dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي), whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي). Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein‑kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, include ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن). The “Other protein kinase inhibitors in ATC” group, encompassing cabozantinib, gilteritinib and midostaurin, belongs to the same protein‑kinase‑inhibitor class and is employed to inhibit kinase signaling in cancer and to modulate immune responses. Gilteritinib and midostaurin are specifically recognized for the treatment of acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد). Collectively, these agents illustrate how distinct kinase‑targeted drugs are organized within broader therapeutic families and linked to the management of various hematologic malignancies.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 88
agent2_snippets.content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors—including dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib—are classified as protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents; dasatinib, its anhydrous form and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are used for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors such as ibrutinib and zanubrutinib also belong to the protein kinase inhibitor, antineoplastic and immunomodulating group, with ibrutinib specifically indicated for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, exemplified by canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, are another subset of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. Additional protein kinase inhibitors in this ATC subclass include cabozantinib, gilteritinib and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin may be employed in the treatment of acute myeloid leukemia. Collectively, these kinase‑targeted therapies illustrate how distinct protein kinase inhibitor subclasses are organized within broader antineoplastic categories and matched to specific hematologic and lymphoid malignancies, supporting precise, patient‑centered treatment strategies.

Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, comprise ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia. agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 89
agent2_snippets.content: The compound 4‑aminopyrazolo(3,4‑d)pyrimidine is classified as an anticholesteremic agent, and within this anticholesteremic category a subcategory called hematologic agents (hematologické látky) is defined. Hematologic agents include drugs such as zanubrutinib, filgrastim, and protamines. Zanubrutinib, together with ibrutinib, belongs to the class of Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors; BTK inhibitors are themselves a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors that have antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity. Ibrutinib is indicated for the treatment of chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن). Filgrastim, also a hematologic agent, is used to stimulate white‑blood‑cell production, and protamines represent another type of hematologic agent. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors constitute another group of protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties. Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are examples of EGFR inhibitors. A broader grouping labeled “Other protein kinase inhibitors in ATC” also falls under the protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents class. This group includes cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin. Gilteritinib and midostaurin have therapeutic activity against acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد). Overall, the taxonomy links several targeted therapies—BTK inhibitors, EGFR inhibitors, and other protein kinase inhibitors—to their respective clinical indications in hematologic malignancies such as chronic lymphocytic leukemia and acute myeloid leukemia, while also situating them within broader pharmacologic categories like anticholesteremic agents and protein kinase inhibitor classes.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 214
agent2_snippets.content: Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors are classified as protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity. Within this class, ibrutinib and zanubrutinib are members; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن). Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors also belong to the broader category of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are specific EGFR inhibitors. Other protein kinase inhibitors listed in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) classification include cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin. Gilteritinib and midostaurin are indicated for the treatment of acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), while cabozantinib is a member of this group with oncologic applications. Collectively, these agents illustrate how diverse protein kinase–targeting drugs—spanning BTK inhibitors, EGFR inhibitors, and other kinase inhibitors—are integrated into cancer therapy, each targeting specific malignancies and contributing to personalized patient care.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2916
agent2_snippets.content: Dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are both classified as BCR‑ABL inhibitors. As BCR‑ABL inhibitors, these agents specifically target the BCR‑ABL fusion protein, a constitutively active tyrosine kinase that drives the development of chronic myeloid leukemia and certain acute lymphoblastic leukemias. By inhibiting BCR‑ABL activity, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib disrupt downstream signaling pathways, reducing malignant cell proliferation and providing therapeutic benefit in BCR‑ABL–positive hematologic malignancies. Their shared classification highlights a common mechanism of action, while differences in potency, spectrum of activity, and side‑effect profiles inform clinicians’ choices of targeted therapy for patients with BCR‑ABL–driven disease.

Asciminib, a distinct BCR‑ABL1 inhibitor, also offers a treatment option for patients with chronic myeloid leukemia, particularly those with resistance to earlier tyrosine‑kinase inhibitors. The availability of multiple agents targeting the same disease provides clinicians with alternative strategies to achieve disease control and improve patient outcomes.
